1 Defendants the Trustees of the University of San Francisco, the University of San
2 \| Francisco, BJ Johnson, Dena Davis, and Nicole Bohn (collectively, “Answering Defendants”),
3 |lanswer the unverified Second Amended Complaint (“SAC”) of Plaintiff Patricia Westerfield
4 || (“Plaintiff”), filed on September 16, 2003, as follows:
5 GENERAL DENIAL _
6 Pursuant to the provisions of California Code of Civil Procedure §431.30(d), Answering
7 \|Defendants now file a general denial to said Complaint. In answering each and all of the
g |{allegations of said Complaint, Answering Defendants deny generally and specifically each and
9 |l\every allegation. Answering Defendants further deny, generally and specifically, that Plaintiff
| 10 |lhas been damaged in any sum, or at all, by reason of any act or omission on the part of
11 || Answering Defendants, its or their employees, agents, or any of them. |
12 AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSES
13 FIRST AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
14 l. Answering Defendants assert and allege that the Complaint, and each cause of
15 |laction contained therein, is barred in that Plaintiff failed to exhaust her statutory administrative
16 |lremedies which are a prerequisite to Plaintiff's claims against each and all Answering Defendants
17 || of violation of federal ADA, federal ADEA, and California statute, respectively. :
18 SECOND AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
19 2. As a second affirmative defense, the Complaint fails to state facts sufficient to
29 || constitute a cause of action against Answering Defendants. ;
21 THIRD AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE /
22 3. As a third affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred by the statutes of
93 || limitations, including but not limited to, those set forth in California Code of Civil Procedure
24 |\§ 340, California Government Code § 12940, ef seq., federal ADA, and federal ADEA. -
25 FOURTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
26 4. As a fourth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that Plaintiff failed to
27 || exhaust her administrative remedies under employer grievance procedures under the alleged
28 || contract (personnel manual) and/or failed to petition the court for mandamus. |

1 FIFTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
2 5. As a fifth affirmative defense, any federal claim for employment discrimination
3 |land/or retaliation is barred because Plaintiff failed to exhaust her statutory administrative
4 |tremedies with the Equal Employment and Opportunity Commission, prior to filing suit against
5 |/each or any Answering Defendants and/or failed to file suit within ninety (90) days of
6 {| completion of such exhaustion.
7 SIXTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
8 6. As a sixth affirmative defense, any California claim for employment
9 || discrimination and/or retaliation is barred to the extent it relies on allegations that were not
10 ||contained in a timely complaint filed with the California Department of Fair Employment and
11 |} Housing against each or any Answering Defendant. “ :
12 SEVENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
13 7, As a seventh affirmative defense, the cause of action for tortious violation of
14 || California public policy fails because Plaintiff has not alleged she was terminated or suspended
15 || fora disciplinary reason. Tameny v. Atlantic Richfield Company (1980) 27 Cal.3d 167. .
16 EIGHTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE .
17 8. As an eighth affirmative defense to the Complaint, the Complaint is barred in
18 || that the exclusive remedy for Plaintiff's claims is the grievance procedure under the employer’s
19 || grievance policies.
20 NINTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE :
21 9. As a ninth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that any and all
22 |\conduct by Answering Defendants about which Plaintiff complains was a just and proper
23 |llexercise of management discretion and/or justified by legitimate, non-retaliatory business
24 || purposes or necessity. aa
25 TENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE oe
26 10. As a tenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that it is uncertain
97 || because it does not allege a public policy based on a statute, a California constitutional |
28 || provision, or an administrative regulation. Gantt v. Sentry Ins. (1992) 1 Cal.4th 1087, 1090:

3 || Defendants, other than University of San Francisco, were not the employer of Plaintiff.
4 | TWELFTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE

5 12. As a twelfth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred by the doctrine of

6 || laches.

7 THIRTEENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE

8 13. Asa thirteenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred by the doctrine of

9 || estoppel. . oe

10 FOURTEENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE

11 14. As a fourteenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred by the doctrine of
12 ||unclean hands. ~

13 FIFTEENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE

14 15. Asa fifteenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred by the doctrine of
15 || waiver. . -
16 SIXTEENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE :

17 16. Asa sixteenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that Plaintiff has
18 |\not sustained any injury or damage by reason of any act or omission of any Answering
19 || Defendants and/or any of its or their employee(s) and/or agent(s). |

20 SEVENTEENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE

21 17. Asaseventeenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that any legally

39 |lrecoverable damages suffered by Plaintiff, if any, were not proximately caused by any. act or

93 || omission of Answering Defendants.

24 EIGHTEENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE o

25 18. As a eighteenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred and Plaintiff is

26 || precluded from any relief herein because she has failed to mitigate her damages, if any.

27 NINTEENTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE .

28 19. | Asanineteenth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that any clair for

> |lexclusive remedy is governed by the California Workers’ Compensation Act, California Labor
3 || Code § 3200, e¢ seg.

4 TWENTIETH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE .

5 20. As a twentieth affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that Plaintifi’
6 |\claims for compensatory damages are barred as to any cause of action for which such relief is

7 ||not available.

8 TWENTY-FIRST AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE _

9 21. Asa twenty-first affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that Plaintiff's
10 |iclaim for attorneys’ fees is barred because such relief is not available for Plaintiff's cause(s) of
11 || action. : Oo
12 TWENTY-SECOND AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE oe
13 22.  Asatwenty-second affirmative defense, the Complaint is barred in that Plaintiff's
14 |Iclaims for punitive damages are barred as to any cause of action for which such relief is not
15 || available. | /

16 TWENTY-THIRD AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE
17 23. As a twenty-third affirmative defense to the Complaint, Answering Defendants
18 || are not liable for punitive damages because Answering Defendants did not (a) authorize or ratify
19 || any wrongful conduct, or (b) have advance knowledge of the unfitness of any employee or
20 |\employ any employee with a conscious disregard of the nights and safety of others. California
21 || Civil Code § 3294(b).

22 TWENTY-FOURTH AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSE

23 24. As a twenty-fourth affirmative defense to the Complaint, any claim for punitive

24 |lor exemplary damages is barred in that the California statute on punitive damages is

25 || unconstitutional under the due process clause of the Fourteenth Amendment to the USS.

26 || Constitution, is unconstitutionally vague and therefore void, is violative of the Equal Protection

27 || Clause, and imposes an undue burden on Interstate Commerce. - 7

«14 | oftany alleged discrimination. Price Waterhouse Vv. Hopkins (1989) 490 U.S. 228.

29 \\Defendants other than University of San Francisco assert that the Complaint fails to state a
